title: podman container clone -f
body:
add the option -f to force remove the parent container if --destory is specified

resolves #13917

Signed-off-by: cdoern <cbdoer23@g.holycross.edu>

<!--
Thanks for sending a pull request!

Please make sure you've read our contributing guidelines and how to submit a pull request (https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/main/CONTRIBUTING.md#submitting-pull-requests).

In case you're only changing docs, make sure to prefix the pull-request title with "[CI:DOCS]".  That will prevent functional tests from running and save time and energy.

Finally, be sure to sign commits with your real name.  Since by opening
a PR you already have commits, you can add signatures if needed with
something like `git commit -s --amend`.
-->

comments.body: @cdoern go to bed! it is past your bedtime young man ...
@baude sleep is for the weak!
I think @vrothberg is saying the --force should imply --destroy, and not doing that will only aggravate the user.
Ah ok, I will make `--force` imply `--destroy` and add a warning if `--destroy` is used without `--force`

> Ah ok, I will make `--force` imply `--destroy` and add a warning if `--destroy` is used without `--force`

Sounds good!  Please warn only if there would be an error without `--force`.
Why would `--destroy` warn if `--force` was not used? It seems reasonable to want to destroy iff the container is not running
Also, we might want to rename if `--force` implies `--destroy` - `podman container clone --force` to me implies that `--force` affects the clone operation, not that it removes a container?

@mheon I do see the use case for ease of use to just imply --destroy when using force but I agree maybe we should rename it to make the functionality clear. 

I can make it `--force-destroy` and then I guess that removes the need to check for implicit `--destroy`

@vrothberg would that make sense to you: have two flags: one `--destroy` to handle non force destroying situations and `--force-destroy` to handle force destroy situations and just make sure they are not both set?
Lets stay with `--force` since we should keep the ui consistent with the other commands such as `podman rm`
I'll push back on `--force` implying `--destroy` then. At the very least, we should log an error when `--force` is used but `--destroy` is not, even if we set `--destroy` and continue.
With all this confusion, perhaps we should not have --force at all, and just have users stop the container first and then clone --destroy.
@rhatdan I kind of agree but QE is saying for parity with other podman features we should have a `-f` of some kind.

@rhatdan @Luap99 @mheon I think maybe the best solution here is that `--destroy` should be implied to be a forceful removal? Or maybe take a `podman system reset` approach and pop up a prompt asking if they are sure and `--force` can override that prompt

I think we keep it as --destroy and --force. and throw an error on --force alone.
--destroy --force : Valid
--destroy : Valid (Fail if container running)
--force : Invalid requires force.
I'm OK with either approach. `--destroy` killing running containers does make sense to me.
I was leaning that way, but that would be inconsistent with `podman rm`
I will keep it as it was originally (@rhatdan 's approach)

/lgtm
